column2: — What's going on?
— she said at the gate.
— Well, grandmother gave me the holubtsi recipe under extreme circumstances.
— How is she?
— Seems all right now.
And what's going on there?
— Marta's having hysterics.
— Why?
— One of the boys isn't doing too well.
— Which one?
— How would I know?
On the screen they look about the same.
— Ah, you mean those ones.
What did the doctor say?
— Prescribed something.
Marta's having hysterics and doesn't trust anyone.
She called their father.
— Not black?
— Miyechka jokingly quoted the grandmother.
— You can't tell over the phone.
— And Lusya?
— Lusya's with her, and she said she won't give her to anyone anywhere right now.
— You think it's the hormones?
— Either the hormones, or just Marta.
That same evening they took the grandmother to the doctor too.
— What is he going to treat me for?
— said the grandmother, getting ready.
— For being a hundred?
— You're not a hundred, Mom.
— Then for what?
— For nothing, really, we'll go for coffee and to the doctor, we'll keep Marta entertained while they keep an eye on her.
We'll be back in a couple of days.
— I don't want to take the cat, he likes it here.
— Don't take him.
— He can't manage on his own.
— Lilya will keep an eye on him.
— Lilya said she's leaving.
— Where to?
— To ask whether they'll let a child into Ukraine without a passport.
— Which one?
— The younger one, Roberto, they're coming here.
— When?
— If only someone told me who and when.
I'm already confused.
I won't leave the cat alone.
— Give him to Mrs. Darochka.
— She's not fond of animals.
— Granny, I'll stay, — Miyechka cut in.
— If you like, I'll even sleep with your cat?
— He likes to sleep on his own.
The cat had no name at all.
Sometimes they'd call him 'ginger,' but only sometimes.
Why he never got a name, no one knew.
They'd tried to stick a Rudyk or a Murchyk on him, but nothing suited him.
And so for the grandmother he was simply 'the cat,' and for the others 'the grandmother's cat.'
Ginger
For the first time in her life Miyechka was left in this house alone.
She remembered the day when they had all first left, put the grandmother's things in the car and stood outside, unsure what to do next.
The house was then, for the first time, left completely alone.
It became clear that the years of its glory had passed.
That there would no longer be a crowd of people here, that they wouldn't gather plums, that everything now had to get overgrown, to become entwined with ivy.
— What now?
— That key was around here somewhere, — said the grandmother.
— These doors lock?
— Miyechka asked in surprise.
No one had ever locked them.
There was always someone here.
What was the point of locking them?
The grandmother then bent back some bit of iron by the old windowsill and pulled out a huge rusty key.
— Wow, — said Lilichka.
She then put her backpack down under a bush and tried to fit this key into the door.

By joint effort they locked the door, and the grandmother, taking the key in her hands, elegantly placed it in her light beige handbag, where it lay for several days, until she began demanding that Maria bring her back, because she'd supposedly forgotten several important things.
In reality she'd forgotten nothing, but Maria didn't tell her so.
It's curious that the key had lain behind that piece of iron for decades, and Miyechka had never seen it.
She remembered it when the new door was installed and a bunch of brand-new shiny keys ended up in her hands.
But even stranger than that first time the house had to be locked was now, to shut the gate behind the red Honda and find herself here alone.
Miyechka stood outside for a while.
The house stood facing her.
All alone.
She felt sorry for it — it looked old, tired, gloomy.
The new terrace was like a dental prosthesis.
Beautiful, but noticeable.
To Miyechka's joy the grandmother's cat appeared from behind a bush.
He silently opened his mouth wide and disappeared.
— Here, kitty!
— said Miyechka.
The cat peeked out and sat down at a distance, looking at her contemptuously.
— Here, kitty!
— Miyechka repeated.
— Come here, I said!
The cat flicked his tail and went off behind the bush.
Miyechka went into the house and, trying not to think that she was here all alone, quickly took fish out of the fridge and cut the skin off it.
She hummed something to herself so as not to mind that all the rooms were empty and that there was no one outside except that damn skittish cat, whom probably only the grandmother loved.
— Here, kitty!
— she called again.
The smell of fish reached the cat's nose, and he ran out from behind the bush.
By hook or by crook Miyechka lured the ginger one into the house and slammed the door behind him.
— You'll stay indoors, — she said to the cat, as he made a dash toward the door and looked at Miyechka scornfully when she shut it in front of him.
With the cat indoors it was a little better.
She tried to turn on the TV, but managed to catch only the local channel.
There they were telling that the harvest was coming soon.
The cat lay on the grandmother's bed.
She photographed him and sent it to Lilichka, so she'd show it to the grandmother.
— He looks sort of hungry, — said the grandmother, having asked them to zoom in on the cat's face on the phone.
Miyechka meanwhile sat on the sofa, her legs stretched out, watching the report about the harvest.
On the screen a threshing machine drove around, a farmer in a white shirt stretched tight over his belly was telling something about hectares and harvest prospects.
He had large hairy hands.
— Listen, — she wrote, — here I am sitting with the cat.
It's getting dark already.
Come over.
— You think I'll just up and come at any call from you?
— Ustym wrote.
— You won't come to me?
— No.
Miyechka put the phone aside.
— Here, kitty, — she said.
The cat sat on the threshold of the room.
Who knows, maybe it felt somehow strange to him too that there was no one else around.
— Come, purr on my lap, — said Miyechka.
The cat looked at her almost with disgust.
— You too, then?
— asked Miyechka.
She was frightened to be left alone, and, without turning off the farmer, she lay down.
Outside the window the dusk was only just beginning to gray.
One window they had cleared of ivy, and the other had stayed overgrown.
Through the ivy leaves she fancied she saw some figures, she covered herself with the blanket under which Lusya usually slept on this sofa during the day.
The blanket was too small for Miyechka, and she pulled her legs up almost to her very chin, forced her eyes shut and, to the hectares and harvest on the TV, began to fall asleep.
The cat sat on the threshold of the room for a while longer, and then jumped up onto the back of the sofa, lay there and occasionally blinked sleepily.
When darkness had descended on the house and the farmer had long since told all his hopes and expectations for the harvest, Miyechka's phone rang.
Through her sleep she saw the letter 'U' there and didn't answer.
The cat now lay at her feet, and his eyes glowed in the darkness.
— Lord, you devilish creature, — Miyechka whispered and covered herself with the blanket again, folded in half.
The cat breathed heavily in the night, and Miyechka dreamed that she was writing everyone a long letter and going to Australia instead of Lilichka.

She wrote, and cried, and wept out all her grievances against everyone and her self-pity over her own hopelessness, over the fact that she had already forgotten what unconditional happiness was, the kind she'd lately felt only alone with herself.
But the tears were only in the dream, and on waking she realized that she was filled with a grievance that hadn't gone anywhere.
In the window headlights flashed from the road and dogs barked.
The cat shone with his eyes, and the TV, instead of showing boundless fields, just hissed gray.
She got up, unplugged it from the socket and saw through the ivy in the window that the headlights were shining not from the road, but from under the gate.
— Maybe some night fishing, — she said to herself and scooped the cat up into her arms.
The cat was sleepy, limp and heavy.
— Kitty, kitty, — she said to the cat.

Within a few seconds all her childhood fears swooped down on Miyechka — when as a little girl she'd huddle against Lilichka's side at night and imagine that in this house, in this world and in this endless cosmos they were all alone, and only the fish splashed in the river, and untethered dogs roamed all the streets, and you could never again ride a bicycle anywhere.

Now she thought about those headlights and the light from her TV: 'Is it better to turn on the light in all the rooms, and let that evil standing beyond the gate think that Miyechka isn't alone here, that the house is full of people and they're all alarmed by those headlights?
Or is it better to sit in the dark and not move?'
So she just sat there and kept saying to the cat: 'Kitty, kitty.'
Something tapped at the window — whether it was the ivy, or the insects that lived in the ivy, or the wind, or someone from that car with the headlights.
From under the pillow, through a crack, the phone flashed.
— So are you there?
— U asked.
Miyechka put the phone face down.
Then picked it up again.
— I am.
— I'm here, by the gate.
She went out to the gate with the cat in her arms.
On seeing the car, the ginger one clawed his way free and, scratching Miyechka's neck, bolted off somewhere into the night bushes.
— I thought it was bandits, — said Miyechka.
— And do bandits often attack you here?
— asked Ustym.
He was in a white shirt, gray trousers and a jacket with patches on the elbows.
Headlights flashed in his eyes.
— And why are you so handsome?
— asked Miyechka, holding her scratched neck.
— It just turned out that way.
— Straight from a date to me?
— Yep.
— I see…
— So will you invite me in?
— I already invited you, and you refused.
— Should I go, then?
— Stop it, — Miyechka pressed her cheek to his hand, — don't hug me.
My neck is all scratched, I'll ruin your white shirt, and you'll have nothing to wear to your date.
They went into the house, now it wasn't frightening there.
The rooms didn't seem like ghosts.
It wasn't so eerie from the grayness in the corridor, from the outlines of the ivy on the windows and the barking of dogs.
— God, it's so cozy, — Miyechka whispered in the dark.
— Why did you say 'no' at first?
— It doesn't matter, — answered Ustym.
In the morning Miyechka made fried eggs and coffee.
The sun broke into the kitchen.
It was paradise, only a little bit better.
Several times she went out and called the cat, but he, having bolted into the bushes in the night, just didn't come.
It was only half past seven.
The coffee was fragrant, the yellow, sunny walls glowed.
The fried eggs sizzled in the pan.
Miyechka ran outside to find some parsley among the flowers.
Outside there was dew, the grass was wet, and every little drop on it shone silver.
— This is happiness, — she said to herself.
— Happiness with a dozen 'if onlys,' but happiness all the same.
Ustym sat at the table, drinking coffee.
Then he got up and took the fried eggs off the heat.
